transcript: I had the worst cystic acne of my life and I really thought Accutane fixed it. A year ago, my skin completely broke out. Not just a few pimples, it was everywhere. There wasn't a single part of my face that was clear. At that time, I was struggling financially. I had just started an unpaid internship and mentally, I wasn't in a good place either. And then my acne got worse and it honestly broke me. I went to a GP first, tried topical treatments, nothing worked. So, I finally went to a dermatologist and they put me on a low dose of Accutane. The side effects were really hard on me. So, even though I was supposed to stay on it for a year, I had to stop after about 3 months. But my skin cleared, like completely cleared. And for the first time, I felt okay again. But now, a year later, it's coming back. And I'm not gonna lie, it's affecting me again mentally and emotionally. But this time, I'm not the same person I was last year. Last time, I panicked. I kept touching my skin, picking it, making it worse. This time, I'm trying to be more intentional, more calm, more patient. That's actually why I started working on my own pimple patches. Because when you are breaking out, you keep going back to your skin without even realizing. And I needed something. Something to stop me from doing that. And I know, a lot of people are already saying, just go back on Accutane or take antibiotics again. And I get it because that's what worked before. But this time, I don't want to rush into anything. I want to understand what's actually causing it. Like what's happening inside my body. So, I'm taking a step back, looking at my lifestyle and my habits. And trying to figure out the root cause first. I'm not against treatment at all. I just don't want to jump into it straight away this time. So, if your acne is coming back or you feel like you are starting all over again, you are not the only one. I'm right here with you and this time, we are not doing it the same way.

caption: this was my skin before… acne, dark spots, rough texture, everything 😭 and if you’re dealing with this right now, I get it. it’s frustrating when nothing seems to work but here’s what actually helped me clear my skin (and what can help you too): first, understand your skin… not every breakout is the same. acne, fungal acne, irritation, damaged barrier—they all need different treatments second, stop overloading your skin using too many actives at once will only make things worse. keep your routine simple and intentional third, focus on these key ingredients: • salicylic acid – clears pores and reduces breakouts • niacinamide – controls oil + helps with dark spots • azelaic acid / tranexamic acid – fades hyperpigmentation • retinol (at night) – improves texture over time fourth, repair your skin barrier hydration is not optional. use gentle cleansers, moisturizers, and avoid harsh scrubs fifth, sunscreen every single day this is what protects your progress. without it, dark spots will keep coming back and lastly… be consistent clear skin is not overnight. it took time, patience, and understanding my skin if your skin looks like my “before”, just know it can get better save this as a reminder to stay consistent 🤍 #acnetransformation #clearskinjourney #hyperpigmentation #skincareinlagos #skincaretips

transcript: Tretinoin didn't work for me. Tretinoin didn't work for me. Not an uncommon thing that people tell me as someone who prescribes Tretinoin nearly every single day. It's a great treatment for acne and anti-aging, but it doesn't work for everyone and here are three reasons why it may not have worked for you. Number one is that you didn't actually use it for long enough. Tretinoin takes about 12 weeks to reach its maximum effect for acne. Improvements in anti-aging can even take longer than that. And even further than that, Tretinoin can actually worsen acne in the first four to six weeks of use in a process known as the purge. So if you've only used it for a month and it's not working for you, you might just have to use it longer. Second is that you're actually might actually be too severe for Tretinoin. Tretinoin is actually a topical treatment that works best for mild to moderate and comedonal acne. That tends to be acne that looks like this or sometimes even like this. Tretinoin is not a great treatment for severe acne, cystic acne, hormonal cystic acne, or acne that leaves behind scars. For these types of acne, an oral medication may give you more bang for your buck. And reason number three is that you're not actually using your Tretinoin frequently enough. Studies on acne have shown that Tretinoin works best when you're able to use it every single night. Benefits start to be seen though when you use your Tretinoin at least three times per week. So if you're using your Tretinoin less than that, like twice a week or once a week, you know, that's fine as your skin gets adjusted to the side effects of it, but you really want to work your way up to more than that so you can actually start to see improvement. Do you use Tretinoin? Let me know in the comments.

caption: If your acne is hormonal, drying it out isn’t the answer. Here are treatments that actually target hormonal acne: 1. Oral spironolactone Helps block androgen signaling from stimulating oil glands, which can reduce hormonal breakouts over time. 2. Topical spironolactone Works similarly, but applied directly to the skin to target oil production locally. 3. Winlevi (clascoterone) An FDA-approved topical anti-androgen that blocks hormones at the oil gland. 4. Isotretinoin (Accutane) The closest thing we have to long-term remission because it actually shrinks the oil glands, so the hormonal signal has much less “target” to act on. transcript: Do you want to know the right way to use benzyl peroxide for acne? This is from a dermatologist. So, benzyl peroxide can be used as an all-over treatment if you have a lot of acne. You can put a thin layer on. A pea-sized amount is enough to cover the whole face. transcript: This is probably the most underrated skincare ingredient. This is panthenol. I'm Dr Valdi and I'm here to tell you why panthenol deserves a spot in your skincare routine. It's packed with hydrating, soothing and healing properties that makes it excellent addition to any routine whether your skin is sensitive, dry, oily or combination. And here's how it works. When panthenol makes contact with the skin it converts into panthenoic acid which promotes the production of lipids that are essential for the skin barrier to function at its best. Panthenoic acid already exists in our skin making this ingredient suitable for all skin types. It is particularly useful for those with dry, sensitive or irritated skin because of its healing, hydrating and soothing qualities. Panthenol also works wonders for people with combination or oily skin who want to add extra hydration into their routine because it helps the skin maintain a good balance between hydration and sebum. This balance plays a really important role in protecting and preserving the skin barrier. Panthenol is also a key ingredient in our smart base which is used in all of our personalised skincare formulas. This hydrating cream helps your skin retain essential moisture without clogging pores. It also enhances the skin barrier function and helps make sure active ingredients in your personalised formula penetrate the skin without triggering irritation.

It’s not that we all suddenly developed “acne genes.” It’s that modern physiology is constantly pushing androgen signaling up — even when labs look normal. Chronic stress Blood sugar chaos Gut and liver overload A nervous system stuck in survival mode All of these amplify oil production and inflammation at the skin. transcript: Alright, let's talk about using turmeric in skin care. Turmeric has actually been used for centuries in South Asian medicine and food. It contains curcumin, which has proven antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. And it has been studied to treat many things from acne to hyperpigmentation. One study actually found that when turmeric is extracted and compounded into a colorless moisturizing cream, that it can decrease the appearance of fine wrinkles and hyperpigmented spots. The problem with turmeric when it's taken orally is that it has poor bioavailability and it's rapidly eliminated by the body. The issue with topical turmeric is that it can lead to yellowing of the skin. The powdered turmeric that you find at the grocery store has an issue with consistency. The concentration of curcumin can vary up to 500% between one batch and another. So here's my opinion. There's strong evidence that turmeric is a beneficial skin care ingredient. However, it needs to be extracted and then compounded appropriately in order to achieve consistent results and prevent yellowing of the skin.

transcript: Maskne is real for sure. I've been seeing a lot of my young patients with acne in the lower half of the face. I take off their mask and I look at it. A lot of it is really dirty. So if your mask is really dirty, please change it out, wash it, or if it's a disposable mask, throw it away. You don't want to keep reintroducing bacteria into your pores. Also, don't wear your makeup under your mask. Same goes for taking off your makeup before going to bed. Please don't go to bed wearing makeup. If you're in a job like myself where you wear a mask all day, I like to end the day where I take off my mask and wash my face either with salicylic acid or benzoyl peroxide. Benzoyl peroxide is nice because it's antimicrobial. It can kill the bacteria as well as clean out your pores and exfoliate. Salicylic acid is a beta hydroxy acid that goes deep into your pores and cleans that out. I'll do one of those cleansers in the shower and then a couple hours later when I'm ready for bed, okay maybe more than a couple hours, I'll apply a retinoid. Retinoids are great in keeping your pores clean. Just a pea-sized amount to your entire face is all you need. Thank you so much for watching. I'll see you in the next one.

transcript: If you are watching this and you still struggle from mask acne or maskne then let's talk. As a dermatologist I treat a ton of acne but mask acne is a little bit different. Number one a lot of the topicals that we use to treat acne can be a little bit irritating when placed under a mask so things like benzoyl peroxide, azelaic acid, tretinoin. A lot of times that skin on the chin and around the mouth gets really really irritated if you combine those and then add moisture and the friction of a mask so that's one issue. The other thing is that underneath a mask you really accumulate more bacteria and moisture than you would any other time and so you need to have something in your routine that's addressing that bacterial buildup and you could use antibiotics but one those require a prescription and two not everyone wants to use antibiotics or they've tried antibiotics and it doesn't fully work for them. In these cases I love hypochlorous acid sprays. This is the one I use in my clinic specifically not for acne. I use it every single day to clean my patients in preparation for things like Botox and surgery and fillers but the way that this disinfects the skin without causing irritation is phenomenal and probably one of the most well-known hypochlorous acid sprays that you can get over the counter is the Tower 28 Beauty SOS Daily Rescue Facial Spray. This is just hypochlorous acid but it works really really well for mask acne. I usually have my patients use this twice a day, spray it on in the morning before you put your mask on and then spray it off whenever the mask is kind of coming off for the day and then do all of your normal skincare otherwise. If you've tried hypochlorous acid for mask acne let me know.

transcript: Have you ever heard of the purge? The purge is whenever you start using a new active skincare product and it increases cell turnover on your skin. This is a good thing. It means that your skin cells are turning over more rapidly resulting in fresh healthy new skin. However, when that happens it means that everything deep down in the skin has to go somewhere. That means pimples, excess sebum, and dirt. So sometimes when you start a new active skincare product like a retinoid or AHAs and BHAs you may start to break out with a mix of blackheads, whiteheads, or comedones. transcript: If your skincare routine works, why do you still have acne? I'm Dr. Kostin, let's talk about it. First things first, acne is a medical condition. It is not because you are dirty. So if someone has told you to just keep washing your face, they lied. Acne is a chronic inflammatory condition that affects the hair follicles and the oil glands. Here's what's actually happening underneath the skin. Your oil glands produce excess sebum, dead skin cells clog your pores, bacteria multiply, and your immune system reacts, leading to inflammation. As a result of all these processes, you get blackheads, whiteheads, pimples, and in more severe cases, you can even get nodules or cysts. Acne is driven by your hormones, mainly androgens, which increase oil production, your genetics, so you can kind of blame your parents, inflammation, which is the body's natural response. There's also evidence that shows that high glycemic foods, as well as excessive dairy intake, can worsen acne. Here's what's happening. Here's where we go wrong. People try everything when it comes to skincare. 10 different face washes, toothpaste, please stop, and lemon juice, which I guess the thought process is, if it burns, it must be working. And when none of these methods work or the acne gets much worse, you blame yourself or you think your skin is the problem. It isn't. Your approach is. Treatment of acne should not be random or based on whatever product is trending. It needs to be targeted. For example, salicylic acid can help to unclog pores, benzoyl peroxide can help to reduce bacteria and inflammation, retinoids can help to normalize your skin turnover, and sunscreen can help to prevent dark marks. For more severe cases, you might need isoacetinoin, oral antibiotics, or even hormonal therapy. Let's be clear about what not to do. Stop over washing because you're irritating your skin. Stop popping your pimples, you're causing scarring, and stop using 15 different products for your face. Your skin is confused. If your acne is painful, scarring, or it's not improving, you don't need another TikTok hack. You need treatment and you need a doctor. Because acne won't just go away if left untreated. It leaves behind scars and hyperpigmentation that stays much longer than the pimples ever did. The goal shouldn't just be clear skin. It should be preventing long term damage. And for that, you don't need more products. You need the right ones.

caption: How to kill fungal acne for good! Ketoconazole is an antifungal medication commonly used to treat “fungal acne,” which is actually a condition called Malassezia folliculitis. This condition is caused by an overgrowth of yeast (Malassezia) on the skin rather than bacteria. Ketoconazole works by disrupting the fungal cell membrane, specifically, it inhibits the production of ergosterol, a key component the fungus needs to survive. Without ergosterol, the fungal cells become weak, leaky, and eventually die, reducing the overgrowth that leads to itchy, acne-like bumps. In terms of use, ketoconazole is often applied topically in the form of creams or shampoos (like 1–2% formulations). Even though it’s marketed as a dandruff treatment, the shampoo is frequently used on areas like the forehead, chest, or back! Step by step: you apply it to the affected skin, leave it on for about 3–5 minutes, then rinse, usually a few times per week. Consistency is key, as it helps keep the yeast population under control and prevents flare-ups. #fungalacne#ketaconazole#skincare#cura

transcript: My friend died after popping a pimple on her face, and I'm going to tell you the story. And before you say no way, I thought the same thing. I didn't even understand how it happened until years later when I was in medical school. Back when I was an undergrad, there was a girl in several of my science classes. We weren't super close, but we talked sometimes in class. She was really nice, and like a lot of college students, she dealt with moderate acne. Nothing unusual. Then one week, she suddenly didn't show up to class, and she missed the next one, and the one after that. And that's when people started asking around and heard something that made everyone uneasy. She was in the hospital. Nobody knew why, no details, just that things were apparently serious. Then a few days later, news got even worse. She had died. And it was such a bizarre chain of events that the campus newspaper actually reported on it. Everyone was shocked. She had seemed perfectly healthy just a couple weeks earlier. And remember, all of this started from popping a pimple. A few months later, someone in our class spoke with her sister, and what she told us explained everything. Apparently, it started with something incredibly small, something people do every day. She had popped a pimple on her face right between her nose and upper lip. At first, it didn't seem like a big deal, but then the area became painful and swollen. Then the swelling spread. Over the next several days, the infection kept getting worse, and eventually it became so severe that she was hospitalized and died from the infection. At the time, I remember thinking how strange that sounded. Then a few years later, when I was in medical school, I learned about something called the triangle of death on the face. It's the area from the bridge of your nose down to the corners of your mouth. The veins in there connect directly to the veins that drain towards the brain, and unlike many of the veins in the body, they don't have valves. That means a serious infection. That area can sometimes spread deeper into the head and cause a rare but life-threatening complication called cavernous sinus thrombosis. Now, to be clear, this is extremely uncommon. Millions of people pop pimples, and nothing bad happens. But infections on the face, especially around the nose and upper lip, are something that doctors take seriously, and that's why that small region in the middle of your face has such a dramatic nickname, and why in this rare case, a young girl lost her life after popping a pimple on her face. Did you know there's actually an area on your face doctors call the triangle of death?

transcript: Have you ever heard of the purge? The purge is whenever you start using a new active skincare product and it increases cell turnover on your skin. This is a good thing. It means that your skin cells are turning over more rapidly resulting in fresh healthy new skin. However, when that happens it means that everything deep down in the skin has to go somewhere. That means pimples, excess sebum, and dirt. So sometimes when you start a new active skincare product like a retinoid or AHAs and BHAs you may start to break out with a mix of blackheads, whiteheads, or comedones. This may be discouraging or disheartening at first. However, stick with it. After a few weeks your skin will start to settle down and the product will start working and your pimples will go down. Fortunately, the pimples that you get with the purge tend to go away much quicker than a normal breakout would. While it's tempting to want to stop, please don't. Make sure you're just using gentle skincare products during this time like a moisturizer, a gentle cleanser, and sunscreen to help you get through it.

Winlevi is particularly exciting because it targets the specific cause of hormonal acne in the skin, which is either excess androgens or male hormones or increased skin sensitivity to androgens. How it works is it's an androgen receptor antagonist. So it blocks the androgen receptor, preventing androgens from being able to cause acne in the skin. Previously, we only had oral medications like spironolactone, which worked in this way. So it's really exciting to finally have a topical option. Winlevi is especially exciting for hormonal acne, but it also does a good job of treating comodonal acne. So those little blackheads and whiteheads. It's also the only topical medication that may actually decrease oil production in the skin. So it's really good for people with oily skin. So what's the problem with it, you may ask? The biggest downside is that it can be super expensive and insurance coverage is really hit or miss on it. The out of pocket expense can be a lot for most people, but if your insurance covers it, you may have hit the jackpot.

transcript: If your skincare routine works, why do you still have acne? I'm Dr. Koston, let's talk about it. First things first, acne is a medical condition. It is not because you are dirty. So if someone has told you to just keep washing your face, they lied. Acne is a chronic inflammatory condition that affects the hair follicles and the oil glands. Here's what's actually happening underneath the skin. Your oil glands produce excess sebum, dead skin cells clog your pores, bacteria multiply, and your immune system reacts, leading to inflammation. As a result of all these processes, you get blackheads, whiteheads, pimples, and in more severe cases, you can even get nodules or cysts. Acne is driven by your hormones, mainly androgens, which increase oil production, your genetics, so you can kind of blame your parents, inflammation, which is the body's natural response. There's also evidence that shows that high glycemic foods, as well as excessive dairy intake, can worsen acne. Here's what's happening. Here's where we go wrong. People try everything when it comes to skincare. 10 different face washes, toothpaste, please stop, and lemon juice, which I guess the thought process is, if it burns, it must be working. And when none of these methods work or the acne gets much worse, you blame yourself or you think your skin is the problem. It isn't. Your approach is. Treatment of acne should not be random or based on whatever product is trending. It needs to be targeted. For example, salicylic acid can help to unclog pores, benzoyl peroxide can help to reduce bacteria and inflammation, retinoids can help to normalize your skin turnover, and sunscreen can help to prevent dark marks. For more severe cases, you might need isoacetinoin, oral antibiotics, or even hormonal therapy. Let's be clear about what not to do. Stop over washing because you're irritating your skin. Stop popping your pimples, you're causing scarring, and stop using 15 different products for your face. Your skin is confused. If your acne is painful, scarring, or it's not improving, you don't need another TikTok hack. You need treatment and you need a doctor. Because acne won't just go away if left untreated. It leaves behind scars and hyperpigmentation that stays much longer than the pimples ever did. The goal shouldn't just be clear skin. It should be preventing long term damage. And for that, you don't need more products. You need the right ones.

transcript: Here are the do's and don'ts I want every patient to know. Do start slow. Begin two to three nights a week and work up as your skin tolerates. Don't skip that moisturizer. Always moisturize after Tret. Or try the sandwich method. Moisturizer, then Tret, then another layer of moisturizer to buffer irritation.
